title: Sourdough country loaf
subtitle: A basic boule for the home baker
excerpt: A straightforward sourdough boule built around a single overnight cold proof. The method is designed for home ovens: Dutch oven steam, minimal shaping, readable dough cues at every stage.
sourceNotes: The country sourdough boule draws on the open-crumb methods of San Francisco baking tradition and the English farmhouse loaf tradition. Tartine Bread (Robertson, 2010) established much of the home-baker vocabulary; the method here synthesises that approach with the simpler same-day levain builds documented in Richard Bertinet's Dough (2005) and Dan Lepard's Hand-Made Loaf (2004).

content.text: A 73% hydration dough sits between the everyday tin loaf and the demanding high-hydration country loaf. It is wet enough to open a decent crumb but forgiving enough to shape without benching tools. The cold retard overnight develops the flavour that a same-day loaf cannot reach.
Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. Preheat yours at its highest setting for at least 45 minutes with the Dutch oven inside. An oven thermometer confirms the real temperature.
Build the levain, 8 to 12 hours ahead
ingredients in a jar, cover loosely, and leave at room temperature. It is ready when it has doubled or more and the surface is domed and bubbling. This takes 8 hours at 20°C and less at 24°C.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}} and {{wholemeal-flour}} in a large bowl. Add 275 ml of the {{whole-milk}} and the ripe levain. Squeeze together with your hand until no dry flour remains; the mixture will look rough. Cover and rest for 30 minutes. This autolyse develops the gluten passively before the salt goes in.
Add the salt, then bulk ferment, 4 hours
Dissolve the {{fine-sea-salt}} in the remaining 35 ml of {{whole-milk}} and work it into the dough. Begin the

. Over the first 2 hours, perform four sets of stretch-and-folds at 30-minute intervals: wet your hand, grip the dough at one side, stretch it up and fold it over the centre, rotate the bowl 90°, repeat four times per set. For the remaining 2 hours, leave the dough undisturbed. The dough is ready when it has grown by 50 to 75% and the surface is slightly domed with visible bubbles.

Turn the dough onto an unfloured surface and pre-shape it gently into a round. Leave to rest, uncovered, for 20 minutes. Then flip the round, fold the sides to the centre, and roll it toward you to create surface tension. Lower it seam-side up into a well-floured bowl or banneton. Cover and refrigerate for 8 to 16 hours.

Heat the oven to 240°C fan with the Dutch oven inside for 45 minutes. Take the cold loaf directly from the fridge and turn it out onto a square of baking paper. Score the surface with a sharp knife or lame: a single arc cut at 45° across the top. Using the baking paper as a sling, lower the loaf into the hot Dutch oven and put the lid on. Bake for 20 minutes, then remove the lid and bake for a further 25 to 30 minutes until the crust is deep amber. The loaf sounds hollow when you tap the base, and an internal probe reads 96 to 98°C. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before cutting.
